questions.question: What does the Hungarian algorithm do in tracking-by-detection?
options: It filters out low-confidence detections
It solves the minimum-cost one-to-one assignment between tracks and detections — typically using 1 - IoU as cost — to match each track to at most one detection and vice versa
It trains the detector
It predicts future trajectories
questions.explanation: Every tracking-by-detection frame does an assignment problem: M tracks, N detections, find the best one-to-one match. Hungarian is the optimal polynomial algorithm for this. Cost is usually 1 - IoU (higher overlap = lower cost). scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment is the standard implementation. SORT, DeepSORT, ByteTrack, BoT-SORT all use it.

questions.question: The Kalman filter maintains per-track state (x, y, w, h, dx, dy, dw, dh) with a covariance matrix. What does the predict step do before any detection arrives at the new frame?
options: Runs the detector to produce bounding boxes for the new frame
Discards tracks that have not been updated in max_age frames
Advances the state estimate one step using a constant-velocity model, increasing the covariance uncertainty to reflect elapsed time
Applies Hungarian assignment to match predicted states to detections
questions.explanation: The predict step is separate from and prior to association. It projects the state forward using a linear constant-velocity model: state_{t|t-1} = F @ state_{t-1|t-1}, and expands the covariance (P_{t|t-1} = F P F^T + Q). This tells the filter where the track probably is in frame t. A larger covariance means the subsequent update will trust an incoming detection more. Without predict, brief occlusions cause immediate track death.

questions.question: ByteTrack uses a high-confidence threshold θ_h and retains low-confidence detections. In its second association stage, which pairs are matched?
options: High-confidence detections that found no track in stage 1 are assigned to new track IDs
Unmatched tracks from stage 1 (no high-confidence detection found) are matched against the low-confidence detections using a slightly looser IoU threshold
Low-confidence detections are merged into nearby high-confidence detections before stage 2
Unmatched high-confidence detections from stage 1 are re-matched to any remaining track
questions.explanation: Standard trackers discard detections below ~0.5 confidence. ByteTrack's innovation is retaining them for a second pass: after stage 1 matches tracks to high-confidence detections, stage 2 takes the leftover unmatched tracks and tries to pair them with the discarded low-confidence detections at a slightly looser IoU threshold. This recovers IDs during brief occlusions and in crowded scenes where overlapping people temporarily depress detector confidence.

questions.question: In `SimpleTracker.step`, the cost matrix sets `cost[iou < self.iou_threshold] = 1e6` before calling `linear_sum_assignment`. What does setting those entries to 1e6 achieve?
options: It gates the assignment — pairs with insufficient overlap become effectively forbidden; after the Hungarian solve, any match with cost ≥ 1.0 is discarded as a false match
It speeds up the Hungarian algorithm by pruning the matrix to a sparse format
It marks cells as invalid for gradient computation in the tracker's loss
It forces all unmatched tracks to die on the same frame
questions.explanation: By setting low-IoU cells to 1e6 (far above any real 0–1 cost), `SimpleTracker.step` makes those track-detection pairs so expensive that the solver strongly avoids them. After `linear_sum_assignment` returns, the code checks `if cost[r, c] < 1.0` to reject any match the solver was still forced to make. This two-step gating — large cost plus post-solve check — is the standard pattern for IoU-threshold-based association.

questions.question: How does SAM 2's memory-based tracker avoid explicit Hungarian-style association?
options: It runs SORT internally
It requires hand-labelled IDs per frame
It uses a Kalman filter

It stores per-instance spatio-temporal features in a memory bank; on each new frame, cross-attention between memory and current features directly produces the mask of the same instance, with association implicit in the attention operation
questions.explanation: SAM 2 replaces the detect-then-associate loop with a memory-conditional segmenter. The memory bank holds the instance's features from previous frames. At each new frame, the decoder cross-attends the new frame's features against the memory, and the resulting mask is the same instance — no external assignment step. This handles long occlusions gracefully because the memory stays even when the instance disappears for many frames.

questions.question: For surveillance video where keeping each person's ID consistent is the primary requirement, which metric should you report?
options: IDF1 — the harmonic mean of ID precision and recall; measures identity preservation over time rather than per-frame detection accuracy
MOTA
Top-1 accuracy
FID
questions.explanation: MOTA conflates detection and association errors, so a tracker with many false positives can hide its ID failures. IDF1 is designed for the 'who is who over time' question: it matches predicted tracks to ground-truth tracks globally across the whole video and computes F1 on the identity labels. For surveillance and crowd analytics, IDF1 is the metric to report; HOTA is the broader academic standard.
